title: practical english usage - Part 248
content:
With stressed auxiliary verbs, word order can change (►   200.3). Compare: If he DOES decide to come, let me know, will you?

DO  sit down. She DOES like you.

In emphatic sentences without auxiliary verbs, we  add do to carry stress.

You HAVE grown! I AM  telling the truth! You MUST believe me!

change their pronunciation when they are stressed (►   315).

sentence sound emphatic, or it can indicate a contrast. Most auxiliary verbs We   often stress auxiliary verbs to show emphasis. This can make a whole I thought I'd pass the exam, and I DID pass.

or is the case.

Emphasis can also show that something expected actually happened, I don't see my family much,  but I visit my MOTHER  occasionally.

I don't do much  sport now, but I DID play football when I was younger.

‘ Why weren't you at the meeting? '1  at the meeting.’ or present and past, or a rule and an exception (‘ contrastive emphasis’ ).

Or  we  may wish to show a contrast between, for example, true and false, This is your LAST chance.

I feel     Your hair looks so good like that!

about what we  are saying ('emotive emphasis’ ).

There are two main reasons for this. We   may wish to show that we  feel strongly We   can use stress to emphasise (strengthen) a particular word or expression.

(For details, >315.) verbs) have two quite different pronunciations: a normal ‘ weak’  unstressed form, Many short words (mostly pronouns, prepositions, conjunctions and auxiliary grammar •  314   pronunciation: intonation a group of words, beginning on the last stressed syllable.                                     -> A  rising intonation is common in yes/no questions. The  voice rises at the end of b  rising intonation Where's the secretary?

What time's the last bus?

A  falling intonation is also common in wh-questions.

Sophie couldn't find him.

Here's your dictionary.

I'm tired.

The  voice falls on the last stressed syllable of a group of words.

A  falling intonation can suggest that we  are saying something definite, complete.

a  falling intonation There are three particularly common intonation patterns in English speech.

A  fall-rise makes questions sound more interested or friendly. It is common in Is this all you've written? (perhaps suggesting I was expecting more.) The first week was good.
